stem: Someone says the thing that gets under your skin. Before you react, what happens?
options: It's out of my mouth before I've even clocked that I'm angry.
I usually work out what sets me off a few hours later.
Depends on the day, and honestly on who it is.
I can mostly stop myself before I say the thing.
I sit with it first now, understand what's this really about, before I do anything.

stem: When you give your time or your money, what's usually going on underneath?
options: I give when I'm asked and don't really think past it.
Mostly it's the quick hit of feeling good about myself.
Sometimes I check where it goes, mostly I don't.
I usually want to know it's actually doing something.
I put it where it changes something, not where it feels nicest to give.

stem: Something's sitting in your cart. What usually happens next?
options: Once it's in the cart it's basically mine, the buying's already done in my head.
I realize I didn't need it by the time it reaches my door.
A big purchase I'll think through; a ₹300 impulse buy just happens.
I usually let it sit a night before I actually check out.
If I still want it a few days later, that's the only time it gets bought.

stem: Where a thing came from, and where it ends up after you're done with it. How many times you think about this when you choose?
options: Honestly, it doesn't cross my mind.
I think about it only when someone else brings it up.
Some choices, sure; most of them, no.
It's usually somewhere in the back of my head when I decide.
Where it came from and where it goes is just part of how I pick.

stem: Picture the last few people you dealt with today. How did they leave the interaction?
options: A bit more discouraged than they came, if I'm honest.
I don't really track the effect I have on people.
Depends what kind of day I'm having.
Usually a little lighter than they showed up.
Leaving people better than I found them is just the thing I aim at.

stem: Someone shadows your ordinary day, your sleep, your food, your screen time. Would they see the person you're actually trying to become?
options: They'd see someone run by their phone and their sleep, and not by choice.
They'd catch me trying, and failing, to fix the same routine again.
Some weeks they'd be impressed, some weeks it's just chaos.
Most days they'd see someone roughly living how they mean to.
My most ordinary Tuesday already looks like the life I'm aiming for.

stem: Someone reaches out for a favour, you both know they can't really return it. What actually happens?
options: If there's nothing in it for me, I usually pass.
I'll help if it's convenient, not if it costs me.
Depends on my mood and who it is.
I usually help anyway, even knowing nothing comes back.
Whether they can return it never even enters my head, I just help.

stem: Think of the last reel or quote that genuinely affected you. What happened after?
options: I felt something for ten seconds and was already onto the next one.
I screenshot it into a saved folder I'll never open again.
Now and then one sticks; mostly it was just good content.
Something I saw recently actually changed one thing I do.
If it didn't change something I do, I don't really count it as learning.

stem: You find out you were wrong about something you were sure of. What's your first move?
options: Being wrong makes me dig in harder.
I go defensive first and come round later.
Depends on the topic, and who's telling me.
I can usually stay open even when it stings.
Getting proved wrong is kind of the interesting part for me.

stem: You're making a call that affects someone else, not just you. What actually drives it?
options: What works best for me, honestly.
I lean toward whatever's easier, then justify it after.
Depends who's watching and what's at stake.
I usually go with what's fair, even if it costs me.
Doing the right thing wins over what benefits me, every time.

stem: It's one of those heavy days, the kind you're mostly getting through alone. In that moment, how connected do you feel to something beyond yourself?
options: On days like that it's just me, handling it on my own.
That sense of being part of something shows up only once in a while.
Some days I feel connected, some days completely on my own.
Most of the time I feel held by something bigger than just me.
Even on the worst days, I know I'm part of something larger.

stem: There's a thing you're genuinely good at. When you picture using it, who's it usually for?
options: Mostly for me, it's my edge and I use it like one.
Using it for someone else barely crosses my mind.
If someone asks me directly, I'll help out sometimes.
I often point out what I'm good at at other people's problems too.
What I'm good at isn't just for my CV, using it for others is kind of the point.

stem: You finally crack something, figure out the thing that was bothering you. What do you do with it?
options: What I work out, I keep to myself.
It doesn't really occur to me to share it.
I'll pass it on if it happens to come up.
I usually share what worked for me.
When I crack something, teaching it forward is automatic.

stem: You've already had enough, but there's still one more episode ready to autoplay. What usually happens?
options: I finish it anyway, out of habit more than hunger.
I only notice I've had enough once it's all gone.
Sometimes I stop, mostly I don't.
I usually notice and stop once I've had enough.
I stop the moment I notice I don't need more, it's automatic now.

stem: Something's genuinely hard and you could just quietly avoid it. What do you usually do?
options: I avoid it and find something easier to do instead.
I give it a half-hearted attempt, then give up quickly.
Depends how much patience I've got that day.
I usually push through even when it's uncomfortable.
The harder it is, the more I actually want to stick with it.
